question_stem:
# Source A
## Autumn Light in the Orchard

I remember the first time I set foot in that orchard at sunset, though at the time I could not have said why it mattered. The trees stood in neat rows, stooped like thoughtful old friends, their branches heavy with apples that caught the fading light. Each fruit glowed a deep golden-red, as though it carried a small piece of the sun within it. I walked slowly between the trees, aware of the quiet closing in, and felt a curious sense of anticipation. It was as if the orchard itself were waiting.

The grass beneath my boots shimmered with the last of the autumn light. Fallen apples lay scattered underfoot, some split open, their sweetness already drawing insects. A light breeze passed through the rows, carrying the smell of crisp leaves and damp soil. The air felt charged, alive with the sense that the day was slipping away. For a moment, everything else fell into the background. The orchard became the whole of my world, complete and self-contained.

I stopped beside the tallest tree and rested my hand against its trunk. The bark was rough and cold, grounding me in the moment. As I stood there, my thoughts turned to the year that had passed: a blur of obligations, hurried conversations, and plans that had not quite taken shape. I realised how rarely I allowed myself to be still. Here, time seemed to move differently, stretching and slowing, like honey dripping from a spoon. The approaching darkness did not feel threatening. Instead, it served as a reminder to notice what remained.

As the sun dipped lower, the sky began to change. Colours emerged slowly at first, then all at once. Crimson clouds gathered at the horizon, streaked with burnt orange and softened by pale lavender. Each shade seemed to compete for attention before fading into the next. I watched, transfixed, aware that the display would last only minutes. Leaves trembled overhead as the breeze strengthened, and the orchard seemed to breathe along with me, steady and unhurried.

It was then that I felt a sudden, unexpected joy. Thoughts of the coming winter, of routines and responsibilities waiting just beyond this moment, lost their weight. I reached up and picked an apple from the nearest branch. When I bit into it, the sweetness surprised me. The taste was sharp and vivid, unlike anything I remembered from the supermarket. It felt honest, earned. Around me, day surrendered to dusk. Shadows gathered beneath the trees, and the branches overhead arched like silent guardians.

Eventually, I turned to leave. The light had thinned, and the orchard was beginning to close itself to the day. As I walked back through the rows, I felt a quiet promise forming, though I could not have put it into words. This was not a place to be visited only once. It asked something of those who entered it: attention, patience, return.

In the years since, that evening has stayed with me. I carry its warmth like a small flame, steady against distraction and noise. Each autumn, when the days shorten and the air sharpens, I find myself thinking of the orchard. I return when I can, not in search of spectacle, but for the same simple reassurance. Beneath its branches, in the fading light, I am reminded that joy does not always announce itself loudly. Sometimes it waits quietly, rooted in the ordinary, asking only to be noticed.

================================================================================

# Source B
## A Practical Account of Orchard Labour

In considering the conditions under which a productive orchard may be established, one must begin with the land itself. Soil that is firm and well-drained provides the foundation for healthy growth. Excess moisture encourages rot, while earth that is too dry produces fruit that is hard and lacking in flavour. For this reason, the careful orchard-keeper studies the ground closely, observing where water gathers after rain and where the soil dries too quickly in summer.

Once the land has been properly assessed, attention must turn to the choice of trees. A haphazard selection is likely to result in disappointment. Certain soils and climates favour particular varieties, and it is unwise to assume that what thrives elsewhere will flourish in all conditions. Apples of a hardy strain may succeed where more delicate fruit fails, while pears and plums demand different balances of light and shelter. Consultation with local growers, along with small-scale testing, can prevent costly errors in later years.

The ongoing care of an orchard requires steady commitment. Among the most important tasks is pruning, which is often misunderstood. Some view it as merely a matter of appearance, yet its true purpose is far more practical. Left untended, branches cross and crowd one another, restricting light and airflow. This encourages disease and limits fruit production. A well-judged cut allows sunlight to reach every limb and strengthens the tree as a whole. Experience shows that pruning carried out in mid-winter, just before the sap begins to rise, produces the best results.

Equally important is vigilance against pests. Caterpillars strip young leaves, wasps burrow into ripening fruit, and greenflies weaken new growth by coating shoots in sticky residue. These threats must not be ignored. Methods of control vary according to circumstance. Natural repellents, the encouragement of predatory insects, and careful intervention all have their place. Neglect, however, invites ruin. A single season of inattention may compromise an entire harvest.

Weather, too, plays a decisive role. Spring frosts can destroy blossoms overnight, while prolonged summer heat stresses trees and reduces yield. Damp autumn conditions encourage mould and rot if fruit is left too long on the branch. The diligent orchardist learns to observe these patterns, adjusting practice accordingly. Records kept over many seasons often prove more valuable than any general guide.

It must be remembered that an orchard is not a machine but a living system. Each year writes its own account in buds, leaves, and fallen fruit. Success depends not on force but on understanding. Those who work among the trees learn patience, for results are rarely immediate. Yet the rewards are considerable. When the branches bend beneath a healthy crop, the fruit reflects the care and foresight invested in it.

In the end, orchard labour offers more than material gain. It teaches attention, restraint, and respect for natural limits. A well-kept orchard stands as evidence of steady work and thoughtful planning. When the harvest is gathered and proves wholesome and abundant, the keeper finds satisfaction not only in the yield itself, but in the knowledge that the land has been treated with care.
question_text:
You need to refer to Source A and Source B for this question.

Both sources describe orchards from different perspectives.

Use details from both sources to write a summary of the differences between:
• the purpose and atmosphere of each orchard experience
• the writers' relationships with the orchards
markscheme:
========================================
ASSESSMENT OBJECTIVE
========================================
AO1: 
• Identify and interpret explicit and implicit information and ideas
• Select and synthesise evidence from different texts

This question assesses BOTH bullets.

========================================
QUESTION FOCUS
========================================
This question assesses the ability to:
• Draw information from BOTH sources
• Synthesise (combine) ideas across texts
• Make inferences about differences
• Select relevant supporting detail

IMPORTANT MARKING PRINCIPLES:
• Students MUST engage with BOTH texts to access Level 3 or above
• Quality responses synthesise rather than list separately
• Credit perceptive inferences, not just surface details
• Reward judicious selection of evidence

========================================
TEXT ENGAGEMENT REQUIREMENTS BY LEVEL
========================================

Level 4 (7-8 marks): Perceptive, detailed summary
• Both texts: Perceptive synthesis and interpretation
• Quality responses at L1 dealing with only ONE text: Maximum 1 mark
• Quality responses at L2 dealing with only ONE text: Maximum 3 marks
• To achieve L3 or L4: MUST deal with BOTH texts

Level 3 (5-6 marks): Clear, relevant summary
• Both texts: Clear synthesis and interpretation
• Must address correct focus of question

Level 2 (3-4 marks): Some attempts at summary
• May deal with only one text OR both texts
• Some interpretation attempted

Level 1 (1-2 marks): Simple, limited summary
• May deal with only one text OR both texts
• Simple awareness, limited interpretation

========================================
SOPHISTICATION MARKERS FOR LEVEL DISCRIMINATION
========================================

INFERENCE QUALITY:
• Level 3: Clear inferences from both texts
• Level 4: Perceptive inferences revealing deeper understanding

SYNTHESIS:
• Level 3: Clear comparison/contrast between texts
• Level 4: Perceptive synthesis showing nuanced understanding of differences

EVIDENCE SELECTION:
• Level 3: Relevant textual detail from both sources
• Level 4: Judicious textual detail precisely supporting inferences

========================================
LEVEL DESCRIPTORS
========================================

AO1
• Identify and interpret explicit and implicit information and ideas
• Select and synthesise evidence from different texts

This question assesses both bullets.

Level 4 (Perceptive, detailed summary) 7-8 marks
Shows perceptive or detailed synthesis and interpretation of both texts:
• Makes perceptive inferences from both texts
• Selects judicious references/use of textual detail relevant to the focus of the question
• Statements show perceptive differences between texts.

Indicative Standard:
In Source A, the orchard is portrayed as a place of warmth and emotional resonance, with the writer reflecting nostalgically on its beauty and how it offers a sense of calm and renewal. The orchard is almost personified with details suggesting magic and memory, such as the trees ‘stooped like thoughtful old friends’ and the ‘hushed lullaby’ of the evening. By contrast, Source B focuses on the orchard as a living resource that demands study and careful maintenance, highlighting the practicalities of pruning, soil conditions, and pest control. While both sources acknowledge the orchard’s vitality, Source A treats it primarily as a place to be relished and remembered with affection, whereas Source B views it as an enterprise that thrives under disciplined labour and methodical care.

Level 3 (Clear, relevant summary) 5-6 marks
Shows clear synthesis and interpretation of both texts:
• Makes clear inferences from both texts
• Selects clear references/textual detail relevant to the focus of the question
• Statements show clear differences between texts.

Indicative Standard:
In Source A, the orchard is described as a serene, almost magical site, with the mention of ‘golden-red apples’ and ‘soft autumn light’ creating a calm, reflective mood. In contrast, Source B regards the orchard as an agricultural venture, explaining the importance of tasks such as ‘pruning’ and ensuring correct soil conditions. While Source A is more personal and focuses on the orchard’s atmosphere, Source B takes a utilitarian approach, discussing how to manage pests and choose suitable trees for profitable yields.

Level 2 (Some, attempts at summary) 3-4 marks
Shows some interpretation from one/both texts:
• Attempts some inference(s) from one/both texts
• Selects some appropriate references/textual detail from one/both texts
• Shows some difference(s) between texts.

Indicative Standard:
In Source A, the writer seems enchanted by the orchard, describing it as if it were almost magical, full of memories and personal significance. In Source B, however, the writer explains how to run and maintain an orchard successfully, including focusing on soil drainage and pest control. Therefore, one text is about personal feelings and memories in the orchard, while the other text is more factual and practical.

Level 1 (Simple, limited summary) 1-2 marks
Shows simple awareness from one/both texts:
• Offers paraphrase rather than inference
• Selects simple reference(s)/textual detail(s) from one/both texts
• Shows simple difference between texts.

Indicative Standard:
Source A talks about seeing apples glowing and how the orchard is a lovely place that makes the writer feel calm. Source B says you need certain types of soil and you have to prune the trees or else they might become infected. This shows one text is about enjoying the orchard, while the other is about looking after the orchard.

Level 0 (No marks)
Nothing to reward.

Note:
• If the quality of the response is L1 but only deals with one text, the mark must be 1 not 2.
• If the quality of the response is L2 but only deals with one text, the mark must be 3 not 4.
• A candidate has to deal with both texts and address the correct focus of the question to achieve L3 or above.

AO1 content may include ideas such as:
• The personal/emotional focus on the orchard vs. the practical/business focus
• The aesthetic versus utilitarian vision
• Reflections on time and nature vs. methods of labour and careful planning
• Significance of the orchard as a place of comfort vs. an enterprise needing regular upkeep
• The role of memory/nostalgia in Source A vs. structured record-keeping and maintenance in Source B.
